Article.ArticleTitle: Source apportionment of groundwater contamination using integrated PCA-APCS-MLR modeling: A case study of an industrial zone in Southwest China.

Abstract.AbstractText: Industrial zones are critical hotspots for groundwater contamination, where multiple pollution sources complicate remediation efforts. This study employs an integrated PCA-APCS-MLR (principal component analysis - absolute principal component score - multivariate linear regression) model to quantify pollution sources in an industrial zone in Southwest China. Analysis of 108 water quality parameters from 23 monitoring wells revealed severe contamination, with 78.26 % of samples surpassing China's Class III groundwater standards. Key indicators exceeding limits included manganese (65.2 %), total hardness (43.5 %), and sulfates (26.1 %). The PCA identified four pollution sources: agricultural activities (36.806 % of variance), hydrogeochemical evolution (28.794 %), industrial emissions (12.645 %), and geogenic background (8.631 %). Together, these sources accounted for 86.876 % of the total variance. Using the APCS-MLR model, contributions were quantified as follows: agricultural activities (34.703 %), hydrogeochemical processes (32.577 %), industrial emissions (18.902 %), and geogenic factors (11.968 %). The model demonstrated strong validation performance, with a mean R2 of 0.858. These results underscore that intensive agricultural practices, combined with natural water - rock interactions, synergistically degrade groundwater quality. The integrated approach offers a robust framework for pollution source apportionment in other complex environmental systems impacted by human production and living activities. It would also benefit for managers and researchers in other uncontaminated regions globally.
